what_it_does: End-to-end integration test for the ears subsystem: it synthesizes a WAV via a local Kokoro TTS endpoint, feeds that audio through the real local STT lane, checks that the transcript overlaps most of the input words, and runs Silero VAD on the decoded samples to confirm speech is detected.
observations: Hardcodes the Kokoro TTS endpoint to http://127.0.0.1:4123/tts
Uses a fixed test sentence about an Australian voice
Mutates sys.path to /home/tim/company so `from voice import stt` resolves
Calls stt.transcribe with provider='local' as the real STT lane
Uses internal/underscore functions stt._decode_to_float32, stt.vad_speech_timestamps, and stt.vad_has_speech
Pass/fail is based on >=70% word overlap and presence of VAD speech timestamps
Prints config values for WHISPER_MODEL, WHISPER_DEVICE, and WHISPER_COMPUTE
Returns 0 on pass and 1 on fail via raise SystemExit(main())
standouts: A self-contained round-trip test that generates its own audio fixture from TTS instead of using a static file
Verifies both STT transcription and VAD speech detection in one script
Uses the actual local provider code path rather than a mock
conventions: Module-level docstring with run commands and verification note
main() returns an int and the entry point raises SystemExit
Lowercases and strips punctuation before computing word overlap
concerns: Hardcoded Kokoro endpoint and test text make the test brittle to service changes
Relies on an external TTS service already running
70% word overlap is a heuristic that may pass partial or garbled transcripts
Uses private/underscore STT functions, coupling the test to internal implementation details
No exception handling for network, TTS, or STT failures
No cleanup or temporary file handling because audio is kept in memory
notes: A runnable integration probe that checks whether the local STT and VAD lanes actually work by speaking a sentence into them through Kokoro and scoring the result.
questions: What does EARS stand for in this project?
What does the voice.stt module expose beyond the functions used here?
How is the Kokoro TTS service at :4123 launched and versioned?
Are WHISPER_MODEL, WHISPER_DEVICE, and WHISPER_COMPUTE defined in stt or imported from environment?
Why was 70% word overlap chosen as the pass threshold?
What is the significance of the 'Australian voice' in the test text?
purpose_vs_actual: The stated purpose matches the implementation: it performs an end-to-end ears verification using a TTS round-trip plus VAD detection.
intention_signals: The file is trying to prove that the ears pipeline (speech-to-text and voice-activity detection) works in a real runtime by generating known audio, transcribing it, and confirming speech is detected, rather than assuming the models are loaded correctly.
novelty: It uses a TTS-to-STT round-trip as a live integration probe, removing the need for committed audio files while still testing real model inference paths.
contribution: A runnable, self-contained integration test that exercises the local STT and VAD lanes end-to-end and emits a clear PASS/FAIL result for operational verification.
summary_for_embedding: An end-to-end integration test that synthesizes audio via a local Kokoro TTS endpoint, transcribes it with the local STT lane, and verifies both transcription overlap and Silero VAD speech detection.
intention_for_embedding: To verify the ears subsystem (local speech-to-text and voice-activity detection) works in a real CPU/GPU runtime without relying on manual audio fixtures.

symbols.does: Synthesizes WAV bytes by POSTing text to the local Kokoro TTS endpoint.
symbols.description: Builds a JSON request to http://127.0.0.1:4123/tts with the supplied text, sends it with a 180-second timeout, and returns the raw WAV response; this generated audio serves as the test fixture for the STT and VAD checks.

symbols.does: Runs the ears integration test and returns 0 on pass or 1 on fail.
symbols.description: Synthesizes the test audio, prints STT availability and config, transcribes the audio through the real local provider, decodes the WAV to float32 samples for VAD, checks that the word overlap with the original text is at least 70% and that VAD finds speech timestamps, then prints a PASS/FAIL verdict.
